# Transcrição: Lesson 32 - How to Break a Bad Habit Once and For All

- **Fonte:** Lesson 32 - How to Break a Bad Habit Once and For All
- **Duração:** 03:42
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:03]

We've talked about a couple ways to break bad habits and now I want to talk about a powerful one for breaking a bad habit for good, right? How to cut something out of your life once and for all. And the idea here is that we are coming back to a concept that we've talked about in the previous lesson which is identity-based habits. And I remember identity-based habits is about the core of your identity, the type of person that you believe that you are and how that impacts your behavior. What's interesting about behavior change when it comes to identity is that there are often identities that we take on that end up shifting our behavior in quite a meaningful way. You know, if you decide to join a new political party then you may start to manifest some of their identity and suddenly your values and actions change almost overnight. If you decide to practice a new religion then maybe you cut out certain foods and again your behavior changes almost overnight. And if someone were to ask you, well how come you don't eat that now? You would say, well that's just that's who I am, right? I practice this religion as part of my identity. And what's interesting is we can take a little sliver of that and we can learn something and use it for breaking bad habits. And this is a concept that a friend of mine named Nira Yal calls progressive extremism. And the idea is we're going to adopt a new identity, a new type of person that we believe that we are, but only in a really small sliver in an extreme way but in a very tiny way. And then we're going to progressively move that line forward. So for example, let's say that you were considering becoming vegan or vegetarian. Well rather than just going cold turkey so to speak and cutting it out entirely and no longer eating meat, well you could actually start in a very small way. You could say, well I no longer eat red meat. So just one small sliver within this boundary I'm going to be very extreme. And you do that for say a month. And then after that month if you like it you say, well now I'm no longer going to eat red meat and poultry. And so now we've cut out you know beef and chicken for example. And so again within this boundary you're going to be very extreme but you're not telling yourself I can never eat meat or I can never eat animal products yet. And then you continue to move forward. And after another month you say, well now I'm going to say I don't eat red meat, I don't eat poultry and I don't eat fish. And you can see where we're going here right. We're building some type of habit and consistency in a small but extreme way and then we're progressively moving that line forward. So let's take another bad habit like eating too much candy or sugar. Well you could start by saying I no longer eat candy before 10 a.m. That's you know the small little boundary and this is within this zone I'm going to be very extreme. I never do it. It's just part of my identity. Why don't you want this candy at 9 a.m. Well sorry that's just like not who I am. Then we move forward to noon and then maybe it's like well I don't eat candy until after work or I don't eat candy until after dinner. And then eventually you could cut out sugar entirely. But the idea is pick some boundary that doesn't seem intimidating to you at the beginning. That you can actually adopt this new identity. It can become part of who you are and then you can progressively move that extreme line forward. So rather than cutting out a bad habit in all cases of your life you cut it out within this

## [03:03 - 03:42]

particular context of your life and then gradually move it ahead. So what I'd like you to do now is click on the worksheet below this video. Write down a bad habit that you would like to break and then come up with at least three different divisions for how you could progressively move that habit forward. The first one should be incredibly easy. It should almost feel like something that you know I don't even need to define this. Do I really need to define not to eat candy before 10 a.m. I rarely do it anyway. But that's the point. The first division should be quite easy. An easy identity for you to adopt and to be strict about. And then the next one a slight improvement from there and then the third one a slight improvement from there. So go ahead and fill that out now and I'll see you in the next lesson.
